title: Why Toxic Resilience Is Keeping Women Stuck (And What to Do Instead)
guestBio: Dr. Katie Pritchett is the founder of KPI Coaching and a former UT Austin professor who specializes in helping women break generational patterns and redefine leadership. She's an expert on toxic resilience and advocates for women's involvement in the AI revolution.
excerpt: What if the resilience you've been praised for is actually keeping you stuck? Dr. Katie Pritchett reveals why 'pushing through' isn't strength—and how women can lead the AI revolution by automating their invisible labor.
metaDescription: Dr. Katie Pritchett explains toxic resilience vs regeneration, why women must lead AI, and how breaking patterns creates better leaders.
keyTakeaways: Toxic resilience is the harmful pattern of constantly bouncing back without addressing root causes or allowing for rest and recovery.
Regeneration, not resilience, should be the goal—building something new rather than just returning to the previous state.
Women must be actively involved in AI development because only they truly understand their invisible labor and can automate it effectively.
AI can give us more time to be human, not less, if we approach it strategically and with intention.
Breaking generational patterns requires recognizing that while your patterns were inherited, your power to change them is not.
Rest and knowing when to stop pushing through is a form of strength, not weakness.
The stories we tell our children shape their understanding of their purpose and relationship with us.

pullQuotes.text: Your patterns were inherited, but your power is not
The difference between bouncing back and building something new is everything
Only we can automate our invisible labor—because only we truly see it

content:
You've been told your whole life that resilience is a virtue. That bouncing back stronger is the ultimate goal. That pushing through when things get tough is what separates the winners from everyone else. But what if everything you've been taught about resilience is not only wrong—but actively harmful?

In this powerful conversation, Aimee sits down with Dr. Katie Pritchett, founder of KPI Coaching and former UT Austin professor, to unpack a concept that's revolutionizing how we think about strength, leadership, and what it means to truly thrive as women in today's world.

The Dark Side of Resilience

Dr. Katie doesn't mince words when she introduces the concept of toxic resilience—the relentless pattern of bouncing back without ever addressing the root causes of what knocked you down in the first place. "We've been conditioned to see our ability to endure as strength," she explains, "but sometimes what we call resilience is just trauma response dressed up in motivational quotes."

This hits different when you realize how many women are praised for their ability to handle impossible loads, to keep showing up no matter what, to be the one everyone can count on even when they're running on empty. We've made martyrdom into a badge of honor, and it's killing us slowly.

The problem isn't that resilience itself is bad—it's that we've twisted it into something unrecognizable. True resilience includes rest, recovery, and the wisdom to know when to stop pushing. Toxic resilience, on the other hand, glorifies the grind and makes us feel guilty for having human limitations.

From Bouncing Back to Building Forward

Dr. Katie introduces a game-changing concept: regeneration. While resilience focuses on returning to your previous state, regeneration is about building something entirely new from the wisdom you've gained through your experiences. It's the difference between going back to who you were and evolving into who you're meant to become.

"When we focus on regeneration instead of resilience," Dr. Katie shares, "we give ourselves permission to be changed by our experiences rather than unchanged by them." This isn't about bouncing back to your old normal—it's about creating a new normal that actually serves you.

Think about it: when you've been through something difficult, do you really want to go back to exactly who you were before? Or do you want to integrate what you've learned and become someone stronger, wiser, and more aligned with your values? That's the power of choosing regeneration over resilience.

Why Women Must Lead the AI Revolution

The conversation takes an unexpected but brilliant turn when Dr. Katie makes a compelling case for why women absolutely must be involved in shaping artificial intelligence. Her reasoning? We're the only ones who truly understand our invisible labor—and therefore the only ones who can effectively automate it.

"Men can't automate what they don't see," she points out. "They don't know about the mental load of remembering everyone's schedules, the emotional labor of managing relationships, or the countless micro-tasks that keep households and organizations running." This invisible work that women do is exactly what AI could help streamline, but only if we're the ones programming those solutions.

Dr. Katie challenges the narrative that AI will make us less human. Instead, she argues that when used strategically, AI can give us more time to be human by handling the repetitive, administrative tasks that drain our energy and creativity. Imagine having AI handle your calendar management, meal planning, or project coordination so you can focus on strategy, relationships, and the work that truly lights you up.

But here's the catch: this only works if women are actively involved in AI development and implementation. We can't afford to be passive consumers of technology that men create without understanding our lived experience. We need to be at the table, leading the conversation, and ensuring that AI serves our actual needs.

Breaking the Patterns That Bind Us

One of the most powerful moments in the conversation comes when Dr. Katie shares a story about her young son. When prompted to complete the phrase "I brought you into this world so..." instead of the typical guilt-inducing endings we might expect, he said "we could have fun." This simple response reveals something profound about the stories we tell our children and ourselves about purpose, relationships, and what we owe each other.

Dr. Katie's work focuses heavily on helping women recognize and break generational patterns—those inherited ways of thinking and being that no longer serve us. "Your patterns were inherited, but your power is not," she reminds us. Just because your mother, grandmother, and great-grandmother approached life a certain way doesn't mean you have to continue that legacy.

This is particularly relevant for women in leadership who find themselves unconsciously replicating the same patterns of overwork, people-pleasing, or self-sacrifice that they witnessed growing up. Breaking these cycles isn't just about personal healing—it's about modeling something different for the next generation.

Redefining Strength for the Modern Woman

Perhaps the most radical idea Dr. Katie presents is that true strength sometimes looks like stopping. It looks like saying no. It looks like resting before you're forced to by burnout or breakdown. In a culture that glorifies the grind and celebrates exhaustion as dedication, this feels almost revolutionary.

"We've confused endurance with excellence," she observes. "But the most successful leaders know when to push and when to pause. They understand that sustainable success requires both effort and recovery."

This doesn't mean lowering your standards or giving up on your goals. It means approaching them with strategy rather than sheer force. It means building systems that support you rather than drain you. It means recognizing that you can be both ambitious and human, driven and rested, successful and balanced.

Your Next Steps

As you reflect on this conversation, consider where toxic resilience might be showing up in your own life. Are you pushing through when you should be pausing? Are you bouncing back to old patterns instead of building something new? Are you letting others define strength for you instead of choosing your own definition?

The future belongs to women who can integrate wisdom with action, who can lead with both power and presence, and who understand that true resilience includes the courage to regenerate rather than just endure. You have the power to break the patterns that no longer serve you and create something entirely new—not just for yourself, but for every woman watching you lead.
